test(connection): content corruption repro + accept_uni race documentation Add two test suites investigating residual signed-payload corruption on v0.27.30 (ML-DSA-65 signature verification failures on live testnet despite #195 read_to_end gap fix being shipped). regression_content_corruption_repro.rs (3 tests, ALL PASS): - 5300-byte multi-packet payloads (4+ QUIC packets) under heavy adversarial network (8% loss, 8% duplication, 20% delay) across 3 seeds × 20 batches × 12 concurrent streams — verifies EVERY BYTE - Non-cancel-safe read_to_end dropped-future suffix semantics - Multi-frame single-packet content integrity regression_accept_uni_race.rs (2 tests): - Documents the accept_uni race between trust/binding task (connection.rs:323) and reader task (p2p_endpoint.rs:4398) — both call accept_uni on the same connection with no routing guarantee. CONFIRMED real but NOT the sig-fail root cause (binding not enabled in x0x; binding bytes 0x07 would be dropped by gossip type check before sig-verify). Investigation ruled out: assembler insert/read (4096-seed verified), assemble_unordered_chunks (provably correct), AEAD (authenticated), frame parsing, BytesMut/Bytes refcounting (v1.11.1 verified), coalesced packet splitting, send buffer get(), concurrency (lock-safe), accept_uni race, no unsafe in receive path. Corruption NOT reproducible at ant-quic transport layer with classical TLS. Remaining suspects: PQC crypto layer, or downstream saorsa-gossip.
Your 323K-message soak result is the final nail: standard QUIC data paths are clean. We need WAN-specific behavior. I'm pivoting to connection migration + PMTUD. Both change the 4-tuple or MTU mid...

fix(streams): detect overlapping chunk data corruption in assemble_unordered_chunks The #195 fix (read_to_end fails loud on gaps) catches MISSING byte ranges but does NOT detect WRONG bytes at correct offsets. If the QUIC layer delivers chunks with correct offsets but corrupt content (a condition observed on the live testnet as ML-DSA-65 signature verification failures), assemble_unordered_chunks faithfully assembles the corrupt bytes into a valid-looking buffer. This fix adds a data-integrity check: when chunks overlap (from retransmissions or the ordered→unordered transition), the overlapping bytes MUST be identical (RFC 9000 §13.1). If they differ: 1. Log a diagnostic error with offset and first-diff position 2. Return None (MissingData) to prevent silent corruption This is a defense-in-depth measure — it catches corruption from ANY source (packet processing, PQC crypto, buffer management, or any unidentified path) at the final assembly point, before corrupt bytes reach the application. The check has zero overhead for non-overlapping chunks and negligible overhead for legitimate retransmissions (identical overlapping data is the common case). Tests: all existing tests pass including the 4096-seed randomized assembler contract test, the adverse-network regression (#195), and the new content verification tests (3 seeds × 20 batches × 12 concurrent × 5300B under 8% loss / 8% dup / 20% delay).

test(crypto): PQC ML-KEM-768 data integrity verification Exercises the REAL ML-KEM-768 key exchange → TLS 1.3 key schedule → AES-128-GCM AEAD path that x0x uses on the testnet. All previous tests used classical TLS (configured_provider returns aws-lc-rs without ML-KEM groups). This test creates a provider with ML-KEM-768 key exchange groups and classical signature verification (for rcgen certs), then sends 10 × 5300-byte multi-packet messages. Every byte of every message is verified correct. Result: PASS — PQC key derivation produces correct AEAD keys. Wrong-key corruption is mathematically impossible with AEAD tag verification (rustls.rs:782 decrypt_in_place returns CryptoError on any mismatch). This rules out the PQC key schedule as the corruption source.
